text: this evening, and Psalm 119 is a wonderful passage of Scripture, a very lengthy Psalm, of course the longest chapter in the Bible, 176 verses, and there's so much within Psalm 119 about God's Word, and I'm thankful to have God's Word, aren't you? And what a blessing to have a Bible. We know that God's Word is powerful, it's profitable, it's been preserved for us, and we're so blessed to have a Bible. This evening we're going to look at a few verses from Psalm 119, and the subject, as I mentioned this morning, and you see on the bulletin, the title for the message this evening, is finish well. And the subject of finishing is often discussed. I think about the idea of finish, in my mind, immediately I begin to think about a race. I think about either a a race where you're running, a race where you're driving, some type of race, and something that is interesting about a race, and something that's interesting about finishing, is that you know where the finish line is. Does that make sense? We're in a race, if we're going to stand up here and say, hey, Zachary, I think I can run faster than you, and we're going to go outside, and we're going to have a foot race, and I'm going to tell him that's where the finish line is, right? And we know where we're running to, we know that there's a finish line. If we don't establish the finish line, then we don't know who wins the race. Does that make sense? If I am going to race against Zachary, and I'm beating him for part of the time, and then he pulls ahead, and I say, well, I won because we already passed the finish line, but we never establish what the finish line was. Or, we don't establish the finish line, and we run and run and run until I get tired because I'm old, and then he eventually passes me because he's young, and he says I won because there was no established finish line. Does that make sense tonight? And here's something, so when you think about finishing, in most of our experiences in life, there's a known finish line, there's a spot, there's a position, a place, a location that we're seeking to get to. When we talk about finishing, as we speak about our lives, and as we evaluate our lives, there's something very important that we must acknowledge, and that is that we do not know where the finish line is. I don't know where my finish line is, and neither do you. Let me prove it to you. And we understand that that was something that was very real for David because of the intent of Saul, and because Saul had made it known that that's what he wanted to do. And David was fleeing, and David really was in a position where he knew, there's but a step between me and death. But I've often said to people that the reality for you and for me is that there's but a step between me and death. I don't know when death is going to come, neither do you. But I would submit to you this evening that that's the finish line. Then let me remind you of this, as God's people, in Hebrews chapter number 10, in verse number 37, the Lord, we have this truth, this promise, for yet a little while, and he that shall come will come and will not tarry. Jesus is coming back, and I don't know the time or the hour, and neither do you. But when the trumpet of the Lord shall sound, guess what? Finished. The race is finished. Would you agree? Think about this, in James 5, in verse number 8, the Bible says, Be also patient, establish your hearts, for the coming of the Lord draweth nigh. Amen. Even so come, Lord Jesus. And this evening, I want you to understand and I want you to acknowledge with me that our life, if we could take the and adopt the analogy that the Apostle Paul uses, really speaking to the church in Corinth there in 1 Corinthians 9, even making reference to it in 2 Timothy chapter 4, which we'll look at in just a little while this evening, that our life is likened to a race. We understand that we can look at Hebrews chapter number 12, and we understand that we're to run this race, and we understand that as we consider the truth of Hebrews chapter number 12, when the Bible tells us, Wherefore, seeing we also are compassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight, and the sin which doth so easily beset us, notice, and let us run with patience the race that is set before us, looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is set down at the right hand of the thorn of God. And so this evening, as we consider the subject of finishing, I believe that it's important that we acknowledge that we don't know when our race is going to end. With that being said, it must mean, it must lead us to a place that I run every day like today might be the last day. You know, I've never, I never ran track. I don't have the build for running track. I played other sports. I enjoy playing sports. I did have to run a number of the sports I played, of course. But in that understanding of running a race, and you think about that last leg of the race, and if you ever ran track, if you're involved in running a race in that sense, you put it all on the line. I mean, you can see the finish line, you're getting to the end, and you want to finish with nothing left in the tank. That's the idea. You don't want to finish the race and be like, man, I could run again. Because if you do that, then you didn't really put it all in there. And we think about our lives, and we think about the subject of finishing, and yes, it's a subject that's often discussed, but some people have the idea, and it's unfortunate, but they have the idea that I have 25 years, I have 35 years, I have 45 years, and I know that I've said this before, but I was here as the pastor for just a few months, and a man came to my office and asked me this question. He said, how is it, pastor, can you give me counsel on how I can serve God in my retirement years? And I promise you, it happens so often that people say, can I meet with you, and I have no idea what they want to meet with me about, and it's always like a wonder what this is going to be all about. And I pray for wisdom, God, give me wisdom, God, you know what they're going to ask, and Lord, help me to be able to give a Bible answer. And I remember when that question was asked, it was asked by somebody that was about a year younger than I was. I started pastoring the church when I was 31 years old. So this person was just hitting their 30th birthday and asking me, how can I serve God when I retire? My answer, serve God today. Serve God today. The reality is that I know that there's much planning that takes place in our society with regard to retirement, and I'm not against retirement planning. But I want you to understand that there are plenty of people that have planned for retirement that never reached retirement. There's a piece of property that's adjacent to our church property. It sits just out there, and the individual that bought that property in 1978 bought that property with the intent of retiring on that property. They bought that property in 1978. They lived over in Falls Church, and they were working and doing their career and finishing their things, and they had a plan that they would retire from their job and they would build a house on that piece of land. And I tell you, just about a year before they would reach retirement age, they went home to glory. I don't know if they went to glory. They passed it. Their life was ended. They never built a house there. That property was sold to somebody else, and a developer put two houses on that property. I'm not trying to say that that's poor planning on their part. I'm just simply saying we don't know when our life is going to end. And so if we want to finish well, and that's our goal, then we must run the race daily like today might be the last day. Let today could be when I cross the finish line. Think about what Paul says to the churches as he's writing to these churches in the region of Galatia. The book of Galatians is written to those churches that were established during that first missionary journey, and the Apostle Paul says this in Galatians 5, in verse number 7. Ye did run well. Who did hinder you that you should not obey the truth? Can I tell you there's a lot of factors that we face that try to keep us from finishing well? A lot of factors. We ought to have a desire to be taught the truth of God's Word. I am so thankful that we have a Bible. I'm so thankful that God wants to teach us and instruct us. And of course, we find that what it is that he desires to be taught is the way of thy statutes. I don't know when the end will be, and neither do you. Psalm 119, the psalm most likely written by David, the sweet psalmist of Israel, we don't know that for a hundred percent certainty. But I would say that even for David or anyone else, humanly speaking, that would be used of the Lord to write this psalm, they too did not know when the end would be. In fact, as I mentioned earlier, by way of introduction, it was David that made the statement in 1 Samuel 20, "'There's but a step between me and death.'" It's the idea of, I'm going to learn the truth of the Bible, and I'm going to apply, and do, and obey, and keep it, and I'm going to do it every day. Why? Because unto the end. But in the race of life, we don't know where the finish line is. We have no idea. So today must be lived like it's our last day. And if it's not our last day, we get to do it again tomorrow. Amen. We get to live for Jesus again. We get to serve Him again. We get to tell somebody about Him again. We get to love Him more and more. Amen. And if today is the last day, if I finish my race today, then I want to finish well, finish strong, doing what He's called me to do. Let's pray together. Father, we love You. Thank You for loving us. God, none of us know where that finish line is. You do and we rest in that. We know that. We know that our life is in Your hand. We know that there's an appointed time as it is appointed unto man once to die. But after this, the judgment. We don't know the day nor the hour. We don't want to. In fact, when it was revealed to Saul how he would die, when he would die, it absolutely paralyzed him. God, I pray that You would just help each of us here this evening to make a commitment, decision, determination that we're going to finish well with Your grace, by Your grace, with Your help, that we want to finish well. We will finish, but how will we finish? God, help us to purpose as the Apostle Paul did. Help us, Lord, to have the attitude of the psalmist of being teachable and desiring to know Your Word and purposing in our lives to perform, to keep Thy statutes that we might live each day honoring and glorifying Thee, knowing that today could be the finished day. Lord, I pray that in the moment of invitation that You would work in hearts and lives. I pray that You would help us, Lord, to just truly examine our own hearts. Lord, help us to not necessarily be affrighted, but Lord, help us to be determined that we want to live for You each day so that we might finish well. We give You the glory for all that You'll do for us in Christ's name we pray. Would you stand with me? Heads are bowed, eyes are closed.

segments.text: this evening, and Psalm 119 is a wonderful passage of Scripture, a very lengthy
Psalm, of course the longest chapter in the Bible, 176 verses,
and there's so much within Psalm 119 about God's Word,
and I'm thankful to have God's Word, aren't you? And what a blessing
to have a Bible. We know that God's Word is powerful,
it's profitable, it's been preserved for us, and we're so blessed to have
a Bible. This evening we're going to look at a few verses from Psalm 119,
and the subject, as I mentioned this morning, and you see on the bulletin, the
title for the message this evening,
is finish well. And the subject of finishing
is often discussed. I think about the idea of finish,
in my mind, immediately I begin to think about a race. I think about either a
a race where you're running, a race where you're driving, some type of race, and
something that is interesting about a race, and something that's interesting
about finishing,
is that you know where the finish line is. Does that make sense?
We're in a race, if we're going to stand up here and say, hey, Zachary, I think I can run
faster than you,
and we're going to go outside, and we're going to have a foot race, and I'm going to tell him
that's where the finish line is, right?
And we know where we're running to, we know that there's a finish line.
If we don't establish the finish line, then we don't know who wins the race.
Does that make sense? If I am going to race against Zachary, and I'm beating him for
part of the time, and then he pulls ahead, and I say, well, I won because
we already passed the finish line, but we never establish what the finish line was.
Or, we don't establish the finish line, and we run and run and run until
I get tired because I'm old, and then he eventually passes me because he's young,
and he says I won because there was no established finish line.
Does that make sense tonight?
And here's something, so when you think about finishing, in most of our experiences in life,
there's a known finish line, there's a spot, there's a position, a place, a location
that we're seeking to get to.
When we talk about finishing, as we speak about our lives, and as we evaluate our lives,
there's something very important that we must acknowledge,
and that is that we do not know where the finish line is.

but in James chapter number four, the Bible tells us that our life is a vapor
that appears for a little time and then vanisheth away.

because Saul had every intent on killing David.

And David was fleeing, and David really was in a position where he knew,
there's but a step between me and death.

between me and death.
I don't know when death is going to come, neither do you.
But I would submit to you this evening that that's the finish line.

for yet a little while, and he that shall come will come and will not tarry.

with this idea of finishing well. The Bible says in verse number 33 of Psalm

"'And take not the word of truth utterly out of my mouth, for I have hoped in thy

that we die in an old age, but there are plenty of people that die in their youth. Plenty.
I don't enjoy doing it. In fact, I've never actually done it. I can only do it in my mind.
I'm sure I could go physically and do it. I've just never had the real desire to do
it. But you can go through a cemetery or a graveyard, and you can look at markers, and
you can find that all those markers, there's no care of age. You go through and you'll

with a man, Chris. His wife was dying of cancer. And I sat in the room with that man and tried
to help he and his wife through that process. And it was a difficulty. They had young children
at home still. And it was just a sad situation. And she had been diagnosed with terminal cancer.
And there was really everything that medical professions could do had been accomplished.
And it just was to the point where there was, humanly speaking, there was nothing that could

died because she had trusted Christ as her Savior when she was a young girl. And that
brought me great comfort and encouragement. The husband, on the other hand, was lost,
and he knew it. And I remember sitting there talking to him, and as his wife would fade
in and out and eventually fade out, she didn't die at that moment, but she just was not very
coherent for the remainder of that visit. And I looked at that man and I said, listen,
I understand what the doctors have said. You've communicated it to me. And I understand that
the expectation here is that your wife will soon die and that you and I and your children
will outlive her. But can I tell you, I might die on my way back to the office today. I
was just in Chantilly visiting with them. I said, I just have no idea. You might die
going to the office tomorrow. You have no idea. I know we don't like to think about
